title: Debt Validation Demand
description: Exercise your FDCPA rights to demand proof that a debt collector can prove the debt is valid

what_is_fdcpa.title: You have powerful rights against debt collectors
what_is_fdcpa.body:
The Fair Debt Collection Practices Act (FDCPA) gives you the right to demand that a debt collector prove the debt they're trying to collect is actually valid. This is called debt validation.

Here's why this matters:

• Many medical debts are sold and resold, so the collector may not have proper documentation
• The amount may have been inflated with improper fees or interest
• The statute of limitations may have expired, meaning the debt is too old to be legally collected
• The original bill may have contained errors that were never corrected

While the collector is validating the debt, they must stop all collection activity: no calls, no letters, no credit reporting.

callout.text: IMPORTANT: Do not acknowledge the debt, make any payment (even a small one), or discuss the debt on the phone until you've sent this letter. Any of these actions could restart the statute of limitations.

lawsuit_check.prompt: Has the debt collector filed a lawsuit against you?
lawsuit_check.subtext: This means you've been served with court papers, not just letters or phone calls.

options.label: Yes, I've been served with a lawsuit
No, just letters and/or phone calls
I'm not sure
options.helpText: If someone came to your door or you received papers from a court, that's a lawsuit. Collection letters from companies are not lawsuits.

need_lawyer.title: If you've been sued, you need legal help immediately
need_lawyer.body:
When a debt collector files a lawsuit, you have a limited time to respond (usually 20-30 days depending on your state). If you don't respond, the collector gets a default judgment, which allows them to garnish wages, freeze bank accounts, and place liens on property.

This is beyond what a template letter can handle. You need an attorney.

Here's what to do right now:

1. DO NOT ignore the lawsuit. This is the worst thing you can do.
2. Look for the deadline to respond on the court papers
3. Contact a legal aid organization in your area (many handle debt cases for free)
4. Search for FDCPA attorneys. Many work on contingency (no upfront cost).

callout.text: You typically have 20-30 days from being served to respond to a lawsuit. Missing this deadline could result in wage garnishment or bank account seizure.

nextSteps.text: Find your deadline to respond to the lawsuit
nextSteps.details: Check the court papers you were served. The deadline to file an 'Answer' is usually 20-30 days. Mark this date. It's critical.
Visit lawhelp.org or call 211 to find free legal services in your area. Many legal aid organizations specialize in consumer debt cases.

Many attorneys who handle FDCPA cases work on contingency, meaning they get paid only if you win. The FDCPA allows recovery of attorney's fees from the collector. Search the National Association of Consumer Advocates at consumeradvocates.org.

nextSteps.text: Do NOT contact the debt collector directly
nextSteps.details: Once a lawsuit has been filed, all communication should go through your attorney or through the court. Do not call the collector, do not make any payments, and do not sign anything.
Collect any letters from the collector, the original medical bill, any Explanation of Benefits from insurance, and the court papers you were served.

first_contact.prompt: When did you first hear from this collector?
first_contact.subtext: Enter the date you received written notice from the collector (a letter or initial communication). You have the strongest protections within 30 days of receiving their written notice.

fields.label: Date you received written notice from collector

within_window.title: You're within the 30-day validation window
within_window.body:
You first heard from this collector less than 30 days ago. This means you have the full protection of the FDCPA's validation rights.

When you send a validation demand within 30 days, the collector MUST:

• Stop all collection activity immediately
• Not report to credit bureaus until validation is complete
• Provide you with proof the debt is valid before contacting you again

Many collectors cannot properly validate medical debts because the documentation gets lost or was never complete. If they can't validate, they must stop trying to collect.

callout.text: Sending a validation demand within 30 days puts the legal burden entirely on the collector. They must prove the debt. You don't have to prove anything.

past_window.title: You're past the 30-day window, but the letter is still valuable
past_window.body:
More than 30 days have passed since you received the collector's written notice. Here's what that means:

• You can still demand validation. The right doesn't expire.
• However, the collector is not legally required to stop collecting while they verify
• The letter still creates a legal record of your dispute
• If they continue collecting without validating, it strengthens a potential FDCPA claim

The demand letter is still worth sending because:

1. Many collectors stop collecting anyway when they receive a validation demand
2. If they can't validate and continue collecting, they may be violating the FDCPA
3. It forces them to prove the debt amount is correct
4. It creates a paper trail if you need to escalate

callout.text: Send the letter anyway. Many collectors will stop collecting regardless of the 30-day window because they know validation demands often lead to FDCPA lawsuits if they continue without proper documentation.

collect_collector_info.subtext: Enter the name and address of the collection agency that contacted you. This should be on the letters they sent or in any voicemails.

validation.message: Please enter a valid ZIP code

fields.label: Reference or account number (from collector's letter)

collect_debt_details.subtext: Enter what you know about the debt from the collector's letters or calls.

fields.label: Amount the collector claims you owe

collect_sender_info.subtext: This is who the demand letter is from.

meta.helpText: Your information stays in your browser and is never sent to us.

validation.message: Please enter a valid ZIP code

important_warnings.title: Critical: Read this before sending your letter
important_warnings.body:
After you send this letter, follow these rules strictly:

• DO NOT call the collector or answer their calls
• DO NOT make any payment, even a small "good faith" payment, because this could restart the statute of limitations
• DO NOT acknowledge the debt verbally or in writing
• DO NOT negotiate any payment arrangement until they provide full validation
• DO keep records of any contact they make after receiving your letter

If the collector contacts you after receiving this letter and before providing validation, log the date, time, and what was said. Each violation of the FDCPA can result in up to $1,000 in statutory damages, plus any actual damages you suffered.

callout.text: Every contact from the collector after they receive your letter (and before they validate) is a potential FDCPA violation worth up to $1,000 in damages.

nextSteps.text: Print this letter and send it via certified mail with return receipt requested
nextSteps.details: Certified mail is essential. It proves the collector received your demand. Without proof of delivery, the collector could claim they never received it.
Save a copy of the letter, the certified mail receipt, and all letters or notices you've received from the collector.
After sending this letter, do not call them, do not answer their calls, and do not make any payments. Let the letter do the work.

nextSteps.text: Log any contact from the collector
nextSteps.details: If they call, send letters, or take any collection action before providing validation, write down the date, time, and what happened. These are potential FDCPA violations.
The collector should respond with validation documents or stop contacting you. If they provide validation, review it carefully for accuracy.

nextSteps.text: If they violate the FDCPA, consider consulting a consumer rights attorney
nextSteps.details: FDCPA attorneys often work on contingency. The law provides for statutory damages of up to $1,000 per lawsuit plus attorney's fees. Visit consumeradvocates.org to find a qualified attorney.
